coredata.dc:title: Investigation of sulfur related defects in graphene quantum dots for tuning photoluminescence and high quantum yield
coredata.prism:issueName: 4th International Conference on Nanoscience and Nanotechnology
coredata.dc:creator: Kharangarh, Poonam R.
Umapathy, Siva
Singh, Gurmeet
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a comprehensive study of the impact of defects on quantum yield in doped graphene quantum dots by having sulfur containing compounds (S-GQDs). The facile and high yielding hydrothermal method was used to process the S-GQDs by selecting two different compounds such as conc. H2SO4 and MgSO4·7H2O containing sulfur. Initially, the synthesized samples were characterized by using High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM), Raman Spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infra-Red Spectroscopy (FT-IR), Thermogravimetric and Differential Thermal Analysis (TGA/DTA), UV–vis spectroscopy, and Photoluminescence (PL). HRTEM images suggest that the majority of the both samples were in the narrow range of 5–20 nm in diameter. Optical properties of the GQDs are altered as a result of S-doping with purple tunable PL at shorter wavelengths. As expected, by using the different excitation energy in PL, appearance of peak introduces additional energy levels between π and π* that provide alternative electron transition pathways. The most remarkable finding is that the fluorescence quantum yield (FL QY) of S-doped GQDs is higher than that of the reported doped GQDs. This clearly suggests that the defects states related to S modify the electron density, tailor the PL characteristics and improvements in quantum yield of the GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
HRTEM
Raman spectroscopy
Photoluminescent
Quantum yield
Sulfur doping

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids confeditor contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb vol volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: 4th International Conference on Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Dr. D.S. Kothari Post-Doctoral Fellowship , University Grant Commission, and India .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (a) sample-I (Mg-S/GQDs) and (b) sample-II (S-GQDs) and insets shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of sample-I in Fig. 2 (a) and sample-II in Fig. 2 (b).
FTIR of GO, sample-I Mg-S/GQDs and sample-II S-GQDs.
(a) TGA curves and (b) DTA curves of GO, sample-I Mg-S/GQDs and sample-II S-GQDs.
Evolution of the Raman spectra of GO, sample-I Mg-S/GQDs and sample-II S-GQDs.
UV–vis of GO, sample-I Mg-S/GQDs and sample-II S-GQDs.

The photoluminescence spectra of (a) sample-I Mg-S/GQDs; (b) and sample-II S-GQDs using different excitation wavelengths with normalized intensity; (c) Photoluminescence excitation spectra of sample-I Mg-S/GQDs and sample-II S-GQDs; and (d) A schematic representation of the energy band diagram and electronic transitions of sample-I Mg-S/GQDs and sample-II S-GQDs.
The Elemental Analysis results from EDAX pattern of GO, sample-I Mg-S/GQDs and sample-II S-GQDs.
Quantum yield of samples using Quinine sulfate as a reference.

head.ce:title: Investigation of sulfur related defects in graphene quantum dots for tuning photoluminescence and high quantum yield

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Delhi, Delhi, 110007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic & Physical Chemistry, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, 560077, Karnataka, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of Delhi, Delhi, 110007, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a comprehensive study of the impact of defects on quantum yield in doped graphene quantum dots by having sulfur containing compounds (S-GQDs). The facile and high yielding hydrothermal method was used to process the S-GQDs by selecting two different compounds such as conc. H2SO4 and MgSO4·7H2O containing sulfur. Initially, the synthesized samples were characterized by using High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM), Raman Spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infra-Red Spectroscopy (FT-IR), Thermogravimetric and Differential Thermal Analysis (TGA/DTA), UV–vis spectroscopy, and Photoluminescence (PL). HRTEM images suggest that the majority of the both samples were in the narrow range of 5–20 nm in diameter. Optical properties of the GQDs are altered as a result of S-doping with purple tunable PL at shorter wavelengths. As expected, by using the different excitation energy in PL, appearance of peak introduces additional energy levels between π and π* that provide alternative electron transition pathways. The most remarkable finding is that the fluorescence quantum yield (FL QY) of S-doped GQDs is higher than that of the reported doped GQDs. This clearly suggests that the defects states related to S modify the electron density, tailor the PL characteristics and improvements in quantum yield of the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Among graphene based, as two-dimensional luminescent carbon nanomaterials, GQDs have attracted tremendous research interest due to its unique properties including electrical conductivity [1], and good chemical stability [2], and novel potential applications [3–7]. Graphene is a zero band gap semiconductor with infinite exciton Bohr diameter and no optical luminescence. Quantum confinement affects in graphene fragments results in a non-zero bandgap which is tunable by the surface chemistry tuned methods, such as varying the oxidation of graphene, modifying graphene oxide (GO), reduced Graphene Oxide (r-GO), and doping in r-GO.

Doping is the most feasible method to tune the band gap in GQDs with heteroatoms to introduce more defect states to the required PL properties [8]. Generally, defects degrade the quality of materials, such as carrier mobility and conductivity, whereas doping provides a means to tune the carrier concentration in graphene, thereby enabling the engineering of novel material systems. Doping with nonmetallic atoms like nitrogen [9], selenium [10], sulfur [11], chlorine [12], fluorine [13], boron [14], and potassium [15] into the graphene during the synthesis process of GQDs is the most practical strategy to introduce additional defects. Some researchers also demonstrated co-doped GQDs with B, N doped GQDs [12], N-S doped GQDs [13] and N-P doped GQDs [14], N, F and S doped GQDs [15], and S-N doped GQDs [16] to modify the electron density and to optimize the luminescent performance of GQDs.

Tang et al. [17] presented a simple approach for preparing glucose-derived water-soluble crystalline GQDs followed by a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method and reported low PL QY (∼7–11%) of the GQDs. Pan et al. [18] reported a hydrothermal cutting method and their photoluminescence (PL) properties to synthesis green luminescent GQDs. Qu et al. [16] has demonstrated that S, N-GQDs processed much better absorption of visible light than pure GQDs and multicolor emission under visible light excitation. Ain et al. [19] demonstrated the red and blue shift obtained from PL by substitutional doping while using different dopants [Cl, N, Na, K, B] for the purpose of tuning the emission energy of GQDs. Zhou et al. [20] prepared a simple and efficient method of the photo-Fenton reaction after breaking CC bond of GO to produce GQDs in large mass scale. H. Kashani et al. [21] synthesized nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) by pyrolysis of citric acid with blue emission and high QY. Li et al. [22] and his group have shown that reduced graphene oxide (r-GO) serves as fluorescent probe for biological applications. Li et al. [23] synthesized GQDs by electrochemical method with green luminescence for various optoelectronic devices. Li et al. [24], in 2016, developed supersaturated recrystallization (SR) method for the synthesis of inorganic perovskite quantum dots (IPQDs) with excellent PL. Here, Li et al. [25] and coworkers demonstrated highly efficient solution-processed CsPbBr3 quantum dot light-emitting diodes (QLED) through balancing surface passivation and carrier injection through ligand density control. Bian et al. [26] observed Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) with bright blue emission by a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment for sensitive detection of Ag+ which exhibits direct fluorescence sensors. Tour et al. [27] reported a facile approach to synthesize tunable graphene quantum dots from various types of coal with 20% isolated yield from coal which are soluble and fluorescent in aqueous solution. Li et al. [28] prepared sulfur (S)-doped graphene sheets by a facile electrochemical method with effectively combined exfoliation of graphite and in situ S doping of graphene together. Luo et al. [29] and his coworkers were successful to observe high quantum yield of 41% and a diameter of 1–5 nm with a clear blue shift for nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots. Gu et al. [30] and his group have used glucose as precursor material to synthesize GQDs through a one-step hydrothermal method which showed strong green PL with FL QY (∼44.3%). Kharangarh et al. [31] reported the remarkable finding of nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) with FL QY(∼49.8%) by using a one-step hydrothermal method. These reported methods are limited by availability of expensive materials, and accessibility of special tools. Nevertheless, the reported different synthesized methods with low quantum yield are not suitable for large-scale production. In order to improve the FLQY, a strategy needs to be defined for doping in GQDs with water-soluble inorganic salts. Although several efforts have been reported, a comprehensive understanding of the optical properties of doped GQDs is still being explored to show the excellent PL characteristics. The FL QY of doped GQDs was reported to be lower than 45% and for the co-doped GQDs; it is approximately 41% [29]. Additionally, it is quite difficult to identify the detailed PL mechanism in each case. Therefore, we need to develop a novel approach to prepare doped or co-doped GQDs with high PL characteristics and high FL-QY.

To our best knowledge, a very little attention for a top down method for preparing S-GQDs has been paid. Until now, blue or green fluorescent GQDs have been reported [33]. For practical applications in light emitting devices and short wavelengths diode lasers, we need wide band gap semiconductors [34,35]. However no purple fluorescent GQDs were reported. It will be very interesting to see the excellent PL and defect states by choosing an element that has similar electronegativity with carbon (electronegativity of C:2.55, S:2.58). In this work, initially S-GQD were synthesized and subsequently cauterised to establish a relationship between the defect states and the quantum yield. A facile hydrothermal treatment for the synthesis of S-GQDs was done by combining graphite powder and two different sulfur containing compounds: – MgSO4.7H2O (sample-I), and conc. H2SO4 (sample-II). The PL peaks of the sample-I were red shifted under a series of excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 340 nm. However the position of the emission peak wavelengths in sample-II remained the same with identical excitation wavelengths due to the more uniform distribution in size and presence of more sulfur related defects in comparison to the sample-I. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) analysis as mentioned in Table 1 also indicate that the atomic ratio of C/O/S/Mg showing that the carbon is the dominant element of the Mg-S/GQDs, and the small doping element is magnesium. The sample-II exhibits small size with 6.38 at.% of S, purple PL emission, and high quantum yield (∼53% FL QY). However, due to the presence of more sulfur contents, the PL of S-GQDs (sample–II) can be tailored from blue to purple. Therefore, sample-II demonstrated excellent solubility in water, intense purple emission at lower wavelengths and high FL QY (53%) in comparison to sample-I.

Graphite powder (Graphite India), NaNO3 (98%, Sigma Aldrich), KMnO4, (97%, Sigma Aldrich), KOH (≥85%, Sigma Aldrich), 30% H2O2 (Sigma Aldrich), and HCl (≥99%, Sigma Aldrich), MgSO4.7H2O powder, and conc. H2SO4 were used as received. Double-distilled water (ρ = 18.2 MΩ cm) was used for all experiments during the preparation of GO and Sulfur doped GQDs.

Graphite Oxide (GO) was prepared by using commercially available graphite powder by the well-known Hummers and Offemann method [36] and our earlier reported work [37,38]. In brief, 3.0 g of graphite powder was added to 69 ml concentrated H2SO4 with 1.50 g NaNO3 dissolved in the solution for rephrase. The mixture was stirred for one hour at room temperature. The container was subsequently cooled in an ice-bath followed by slow addition of 9.00 g of KMnO4 while stirring the contents vigorously by a magnetic stirrer for 15 min. The content was then allowed to warm up to room temperature naturally. Double-distilled water was then added slowly in two aliquots of 138 ml and 420 ml at about 15 min intervals. Subsequently, 7.5 ml of 30% H2O2 was added and the color of the suspension changed from light yellow to brown, resulting in the formation of graphite oxide (GO). The product (GO) was then separated by centrifugation, washed with warm water and ethanol several times and dried at 50 °C for 12 h.

Here, some sulfur-containing compounds were used to reduce GO in water. At first, 20 mg of GO powder was dispersed in 20 ml of distilled water (1:1). The prepared solution was stirred for 30 min and ultrasonication was carried out in sonicator for another 30 min. Then 24.6 mg of MgSO4.7H2O powder was added to the aqueous solution under the assistance of powerful ultrasonication or stirring for next 30 min The pH was adjusted to 9 with KOH, and the resultant solution was then transferred into a teflon lined autoclave and heated at 110 °C for 5 h by hydrothermal treatment. Furthermore, this solution was naturally brought down to room temperature and subsequently the Mg-S/GQDs were collected by filtration through a 0.22 μm teflon membrane. The final resultant solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm before any further characterizations were carried out. The same procedure was followed to prepare one more sample where sulfur was used as a reducing agent. The sample S-GQDs (sample–II) were prepared with a similar hydrothermal method by using conc. H2SO4 instead of MgSO4.7H2O powder. For preparation of S-GQDs (sample-II), 5 ml of conc. H2SO4 was selected to the aqueous GO solution at the same reaction temperature with the same method. Our similar work has been reported earlier by choosing different water-soluble inorganic salts [37–39].

The morphology and size distribution of the prepared samples were examined by Transmission Electron Microscopy(TEM) (FEI Technai G2 T30 electron microscope). The samples were prepared by drop casting the sample solution onto a carbon coated copper grid, followed by drying at the room temperature. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collecte using Fluoromax-4Spectro-fluorometer. The presence of functional groups in samples was confirmed by Fourier Transform Infrared spectrum (FTIR) using PerkinElmer Spectrum RXI −Mid IR operating with a resolution of 1 cm−1 in the frequency range of 400–4000 cm−1. Thermogravimetric and Differential Thermal analysis (TGA/DTA) were carried out using a Thermal analysis (Perkin Elmer) at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from 300 °C to 800 °C in Ar. Raman spectra were recorded using a Renishaw Laser Raman spectrometer through microscope system. UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous solutions of samples were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer, with a slit width of 2 nm and scan speed of 240 nm/min.

The quantum yields of sulfur doped GQDs in water were estimated by using the following equation, Eq. (1) [40].
(1)
ϕ X
=
ϕ R
(
I X
I R
)
(
η X
2
η R
2
⁡
)
(
A R
A X
)
where Φ, is the quantum yield, I, is the measured integrated emission intensity, η, is the refractive index of the solvent, and A, is the optical density. For these aqueous solutions, ηX/ηR = 1. The subscript “R” refers to a reference with known quantum yield and “X” represents the samples. QY of quinine sulfate (QS, QY = 54.0%) was selected as the reference [41].

The two different samples (sample-I and sample-II) of sulfur doped GQDs by choosing two different sulfur containing compounds were synthesized hydrothermally with temperature 110 °C for five hours in a single step as shown in Fig. 1 .

HRTEM images of the both sample-I (Mg-S/GQDs) and sample-II (S-GQDs) are shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b) respectively. The majority of the S-GQDs are estimated to be in the narrow range of 5–20 nm in diameter for both the samples. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns are shown in inset of Fig. 2(a) and (b). The bright points corresponding to the planes of the quantum structures of S-GQDs (inset of Fig. 2(a) and (b)) clearly reveal the transformation of amorphous QDs to be crystalline nature in sample-I and sample-II. The insets of Fig. 2(b) reveal the distance between the lattice fringes of d = 0.21 nm in sample-II, which corresponds with the in-plane lattice that is consistent with many other reported GQDs prepared by bottom-up methods [42]. The observed lattice spacing of d = 0.32 nm was observed in sample-I (shown in inset of Fig. 2(a)), which corresponds to (002) planes, respectively. The observed planes are in good agreement with the earlier reported [43,44]. These results reveal the introduction of escalated density of defect states produced by sulfur into the hexagonal structure. When the density of defects related to sulfur is higher, the observed short distance is adequate in sample-II as compared to sample-I for electrons and holes to recombine easily due to unavailability of excitation light source after doping.

The results of the Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) analysis of Carbon (C), Oxygen (O), Magnesium (Mg) and Sulfur (S) elements for sample-I and sample-II are listed in Table-1. The degree of reduction of GQDs can be found by the ratio of O/C and (O + S)/C ratio in sample-I and sample-II. It can be expected that the C contents of the GQDs after doping increased due to reduction treatment of partial oxygen and doped materials containing functional groups. Sample-I (Mg-S/GQDs) shows an O/C ratio of 0.23, while the ratio of (O + S)/C in sample-I is 0.28. Similarly, the ratios of O/C and (O + S)/C in sample-II were found to be 0.27 and 0.36 respectively. These values indicated that more sulfur contents were present in sample-II as compared to sample-I which reduced the degree of conjugation in GQDs. Therefore, we considered the determination of the ratio of (O + S)/C to be an alternate way to judge the degree of reduction of GQDs in place of the measurements of the O/C ratio. Sulfur readily reacted with the present functional groups in GQDs to form the sulphoxide and sulfones, which were mainly responsible for the incorporation of sulfur. (EDAX) analysis as mentioned in Table I indicates that the atomic ratio of C/O/S/Mg showing that the carbon is the dominant element of doped GQDs and the small doping element is magnesium.

Fig. 3 shows the typical Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of GO, sample-I (Mg-S/GQDs) and sample-II (S-GQDs). The pristine GO shows the presence of CO (νCO at 1040 cm−1), COH (ν CO at 1389 cm−1), CO (νCO at 1639 cm−1 and 1726 cm−1), and CH stretch (νCO at 2901–2986 cm−1) which are similar as earlier reported in our previous work [37–39]. After hydrothermal treatment with MgSO4·7H2O, sample-I shows the strong vibrational bands at 584, 670, 881, 1163, 1392, 1531, 1643, 2382, and 3417 cm−1, while the peaks were observed in sample −II are at 576, 858, 1018, 1066, 1186, 1282, 1564, 1732, 2357, and 3465 cm−1. The emerging peaks of sulfur doped GQDs (shown in sample-I and sample-II) at 570–670 cm−1 (bond formation of CSC) indicate the effect of doping of sulfur in the carbon matrix [32]. Moreover, the bands at 800–950 cm−1 indicate the bond formation of S-OR (esters) and S-S (disulphide), respectively [44]. The presence of sulfur is further confirmed based on the observed peaks of 1150–1300 cm−1 (CO stretch of COOH or SO stretch of sulfones) suggest the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups [44–48]. The strong peak in the range of 1000–1130 cm−1 contains the contributions from the SO stretch (sulphoxide) and CO stretch (alcohol group). The strong absorptions at 3465 cm−1 (CH) peak in sample-I suggest that the presence of hydrogen content after hydrothermal treatment, which is clearly seen in sample-II with less intense peak. It should be noted that an increase in intensity of band at the 3465 cm−1 in sample-II was observed due to the presence of the oxygen-containing functional groups and defects. In addition, after doping the peak corresponding to carboxyl CO stretches at 1392 cm−1 downshifted with an increased intensity, which is a testament to carboxylic acid coordination to Mg2+ ions [49,50]. These results confirm the formation of the more dominant excellent quality of S as compared to Mg in GQDs.

The thermal stabilities of synthesized GO, Mg-S/GQDs (sample-I) and S-GQDs (sample-II) were studied by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DTA), which are shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b) respectively. The two major steps of weight loss from TGA plots were observed for GO, and sample-I; while sample-II involved in three steps, were shown in Fig. 4(a). The first step, in TGA plot, a weight loss of an approximately 17% between 70 °C and 125 °C was seen in GO and sample-I. This was due to the loss of H2O molecules in the GO sheet layers, which correspond to strong exothermic peaks at 100 °C in GO and weak endothermic peak at 100 °C in sample-I in the respective DTA curve (see Fig. 4(b)). For sample-II, a small weight loss (∼2.6%) takes place below 200 °C, which is caused by the desorption of physisorbed water molecules and the exothermic DTA peaks indicates that the loss of H2O molecules was observed at ∼150 °C, respectively. It is also worth noting that in the DTA curve of sample-II, loss of H2O molecules peak is evolved into a clear double peak at around 50 °C and 70 °C, which infers that sample-II is good in thermal stability. This might be due to the presence of more concentration of sulfur defects; sample-II has better graphitization and deoxygenation with enhanced Vander Walls interactions between layers. The second step involves the weight loss of ∼45% in GO, ∼36.73% in sample (I) and ∼17% in sample-II in the temperature range of 250–470 °C. In contrast to sample-I and sample-II, GO sample does not demonstrate peak in the range before carbon burning (T < 350 °C) implying that GO almost liberates at high temperature. However, there is a gradual decrease in the TGA curve of sample-I and sample-II and carbon burning at around 470–540 °C, which corresponds to the exothermic peaks in the respective DTA curve at 461 and 525 °C. In the third step, sample–II shows a gradual decrease in the TGA curve at 662 °C with weight loss of ∼56% indicating the efficient removal of more stable oxygen functionalities after reduction by sulfur containing compounds. It is interesting to note that the thermal stability of sample-II is much better than that of GO and sample-I. Also to further confirmation for the effect of sulfur in sample-II, the DTA of sample-II mainly comprises three signals: the first one corresponds to the β transition of the sulfur (β-sulfur) at <100 °C (around ∼50 °C, and ∼70 °C), the second is the λ-transition [42] which starts at ∼150 °C and the third is the sulfur within the doped GQDs was completely sublimated at ∼330 °C. These results are clearly evidenced by the small exothermic peak of the DTA (shown in Fig. 4(b)) which is consistent with the reported work [45].

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful method that can tune the band gap for carbon materials to investigate the defects and doping [51]. Raman spectra of GO, sample-I (Mg-S/GQDs), sample-II (S-GQDs) are shown in Fig. 5 . Two prominent bands for GO at around 1358 cm−1 and 1607 cm−1 are assigned to the well-defined D and G bands, respectively. The prominent G peaks located at 1597 cm−1 and a slightly less intense D band at 1346 cm−1 is observed in sample-I. While the location of G peak does not change, there is a small shift of D peaks located at 1353 cm−1 in sample-II. The D band is attributed to the disordered nature due to heteroatoms doping defects and reduction in size and the G band is related to the raman active E2g mode and measures the crystalline nature of a carbon material [37]. Due to increase in sulfur related defects, the shift in D band from GO to Mg-S/GQDs sample-I and S-GQDs sample-II, towards lower wavenumber reveals the more disordered structure involving carbon-sulfur bond. The observed D and G bands in sample-I and sample-II are much broader than GO due to the presence of more defects. The observed Intensity (ID/IG) ratio of the sample-I is 0.99, while in sample-II, this ratio is 1.02 which is higher than GO (0.84) [37]. The increased intensity ratio in both samples (I and II) is a measure of enhanced structural distortion caused by S doping within the graphene lattice. The increase in concentration of defects in doped samples of GQDs after hydrothermal method is not only due to doping, but also due to reduction in size [51]. It also suggests that the intercalation of S atoms in conjugated carbon, which has more defects on surface of GQDs, may possibly improve the electronic and chemical properties of GQDs.

Interestingly, in our case the Raman spectrum of these samples also show a downward shift of 5 cm−1 after S doping, which is consistent with the results obtained in doped graphene samples [46] and further confirms the defect formation of GQDs. These studies reveal that the adsorption of sulfur related defects over the graphene with donor atoms leads to downshifting and stiffening of the G-band [43]. To do more confirmation of these defects, the present study of the sulfur- doped materials (sample-I and sample-II) has Raman shift peak at around 588.3 cm−1 due to an in-plane bending of thiophene which is consistent to the reported value [52]. In sample I, one more peak at 959 cm−1 appeared; this might be due to the presence of Mg2+ ions.

Fig. 6 shows the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of GO, Mg-S/GQDs sample-I and S-GQDs sample-II in aqueous solutions. The absorption peaks were observed at 232 nm and at 286 nm in GO. The observed absorption peak at 232 nm is assigned to π-π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds and the peak at 286 nm is resulted from n-π* transitions of CO bond [52]. In case of sample-I, weak broad bands are observed at ∼237 nm and ∼277 nm, which is red-shifted from 232 nm of GQDs [53] and the blue shift is observed in sample-II for the absorption peak is assigned at 203 nm wavelength. The presence of n-band is possibly related to defects related to doped material or oxygen related functional groups, which exist between valence band (π band) and conduction band (π* band). This is explained in energy band diagram in Fig. 7 (d) by the introduction of sulfur and magnesium related energy bands into the GQDs, which shift the energy levels of the transitions.

Also, Fig. 6 shows that the absorption peaks for GO was identified in straight line, but doped GQDs were identified by slanted lines. As we know, edges of nanometer-size graphene fragments play an important role in giving unconventional electronic structure GQDs; (edge-bound nanometer size graphene pieces) due to their quantum confinement and edge effect. The formation of the GQDs depends on how the sub-micrometer domain structure of the sp2 carbons, present in the carbonized pitch fiber structure, is broken down. After doping with different compounds, the optical absorption edge in the quantum structures of graphene as compared to GO were observed in the absorption spectrum. However, GO consist of individual sheets of graphene decorated with oxygen functional groups on both the basal planes and edges. GQD’s edges seem to be predominantly parallel to the zigzag orientation, but in GO, carbon atoms are covalently bonded with oxygen functional groups are sp3 hybridized.

To understand the optical properties of sample I Mg-S/GQDs and sample II S-GQDs, a detailed study of PL has been carried out at room temperature are shown in Fig. 7(a) and (b) respectively. In the PL spectrum, emission spectrum and quantum yield of sample-I and sample-II were examined using PL by recording at various excitation wavelengths from 240 nm to 340 nm with an interval of 50 nm. Fig. 7(a) and (b) display the various normalized PL spectra recorded for sample-I and sample-II in aqueous medium by exciting at different wavelengths. It can be understood that these samples show the maximum PL emission at 392 nm for sample-I and 420 nm for sample-II respectively which are dependent of excitation wavelengths. This suggests a uniform particle size distribution is found to be more in sample-II as compared to sample-I. It has been found from Fig. 7(b) that the sample I shows a PL peak with purple emission at approximately 386 nm, and 395 nm with the excitation at 240 nm and 290 nm while the observed PL peak of sample I exhibit blue emission at 420 nm on excitation at 340 nm. It might be due to the size effect and localization of electron hole pair of isolated sp2 clusters [51]. As the excitation wavelengths changed from 240 to 340 nm in sample-II, the PL peak shifts towards a longer wavelengths by approximately 25 nm.

It is known that defects states generated by the sulfur and magnesium doped GQDs create additional energy levels and electronic transitions. We have observed that both our samples exhibit dependence of excitation wavelength of PL [53]. The PL of sample-I exhibits the same behavior on excitation at lower wavelengths. While moving at higher excitation wavelengths, the PL spectrum of sample-I shows a purple to blue emission. The observed energy level at 392 nm (∼3.2 eV) between the surface state and π–π* clearly shows the presence of sulfur related defect in sample-II, which causes the purple PL emission from π*-n to n-π [53]. It may attribute to the relatively high surface defect concentration of the high S content, which can trap more excitons and lead to the purple emission. The excitons are subsequently released to surface state by vibration relaxation, and they generate the purple emission. Thus, the present work will expand the potential applications of GQDs in bio-imaging and optoelectronic devices.

In addition, the introduced energy levels under excitations for sample-I were observed at 386 nm (∼3.2 eV), 395 nm (∼3.1 eV), 420 nm (∼2.96) while for sample-II they were at 392 nm (∼3.2 eV), 392 nm (∼3.2 eV), and 395 nm (∼3.1 eV). These levels generate the blue PL emission when they are released to surface states. Furthermore, the change in band structure leads to the change in light absorption and PL properties. The PLE spectra of these two samples were shown in Fig. 7(c). The PLE spectrum exhibits a common peak at 301 nm in both the samples, while a second peak was observed at 380 nm for sample-II.

It can be inferred from the above observations that the defects related to sulfur and magnesium altered the energy level and structure of the doped GQDs. A schematic representation of different electronic transitions as seen under different PL excitations for both cases is shown in Fig. 7(d). The individual transitions are indicated by arrows in the energy band diagram. The doping of Mg and S atoms greatly increases the FL intensity by introduction of additional “defect sites” thereby additional energy levels, effectively creating new electron transition pathways in the band structure of GQDs.

The presence of defects can significantly impact the FL QYs since defect states play an important role for multicolor emitting PL of doped-GQDs. The reported FL QYs are generally less than 50% if there are only oxygenated groups (OH and COOH) [16]. Using Quinine sulfate as a reference, the FL quantum yield of both samples was measured. As shown in Table 2 , FL QY is higher than that of reported for GQDs (∼49.8%) [31]. Interestingly, the incorporation of sulfur atoms into GQDs greatly increased the purple PL to introduce more “defect sites” representing an additional energy level between excited states and ground states. This resulted in a new electron transition in the band structure of GQDs as compared to Mg. In addition, carbon atoms occupied by the doping atoms related to sulfur also reduced the agminated aromatic-rich carbon domains, leading to the increase of the FL QY because of the similar electronegativity for sulfur and carbon. Therefore, the FL QY of sample-II is found to be much higher than that of the sample-I.

In summary, by employing a facile and high-yield hydrothermal method, we have synthesized doped GQDs using low-cost source materials conc. H2SO4and MgSO4·7H2O powder. The doped GQDs exhibit purple PL under the 365 nm excitation UV light. We have achieved up to 53% FL QY for sulfur doped GQDs which are much higher than that of the reported PL for conventional GQDs. It is inferred that the surface defect states that led to the bandgap variation played a significant role in the PL of GQDs with cyclic aromatic hydrocarbon-like conjugated structure. The number of disorder induced states within the conduction band minima and valence band maxima (π-π* gap) decreases, and therefore the number of cluster like states are formed. The effective doping of sulfur in GQDs introduces the additional energy levels related to sulfur between π-π* of C, which vary the electron transition pathways. The electron hole recombination among the sp2 cluster like states may exhibit purple PL at shorter wavelength. Thus, it is reasonable to believe that these high yield S-GQDs will be useful for future multi-functional applications in the fields of optoelectronics, LED emission, bio-imaging, and beyond.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Dr. D.S. Kothari Post-Doctoral Fellowship, University Grant Commission, and India.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: K. Hochi, T. Kubo, T. Sakai, T. Kojima, US 20160293955 A1, Sulfur-based positive-electrode active material and lithium-ion secondary battery, Oct 6, 2016.
